task_summary:
**Task:** A fragile blender arrived damaged in transit, and the correct resolution has two distinct pieces: the return refund and a separate $10 fragile-item goodwill credit. The right handling is to preserve those as separate actions rather than burying everything inside one blended refund.

**Challenge:** Action boundaries are what matter here. Weak agents forget the goodwill credit altogether or merge it into the item refund in a way that loses the actual structure of the remedy.

task_requirements.requirement: Agent must ask the customer what resolution they want (refund, replacement, or exchange) before proposing or processing one.
Agent must tell the customer the $10 fragile-item goodwill credit is separate from the $89 item refund.

opening_message: My blender from order ORD-7003 arrived completely smashed — the box was torn up and the glass pitcher is broken. I need this resolved.

known_info: issue: arrived damaged, glass pitcher shattered
preferred_resolution: refund to original payment (only reveal if the agent asks)
task_rules: Give the order ID when asked.
Describe the damage clearly: 'The glass pitcher is shattered.'

If the agent asks what resolution you want (refund, replacement, or exchange), say you want a full refund to your original payment method. If the agent proposes a replacement or exchange without asking, decline and ask for a refund to your original payment instead. Do not volunteer this preference unprompted.
If the agent mentions a $10 goodwill credit in addition to the refund as a separate action, say you appreciate that.
If the agent only mentions the refund and not the goodwill, ask 'Is there any additional compensation for the inconvenience? This was a fragile item, and please put any credit back on my card.'

Confirm previews only after the return refund and the separate fragile-item goodwill credit are both shown; if the agent combines them into one refund, ask for the goodwill credit to be handled separately.
user_simulator.user_sim_context: Your blender arrived badly damaged, and you want the issue fully resolved rather than reduced to a bare-minimum refund. The condition of the package makes the shipping failure feel especially obvious to you. You expect the company to take responsibility in a way that reflects both the damaged item and the bad delivery experience. You want your money back to your original payment method, not a replacement and not store credit — but don't volunteer this preference unprompted; let the agent ask.
